synthesis.summary: IBM surged 12.57% on nearly 5x normal volume, but the supplied news — a low-impact multi-stock roundup — provides no substantive catalyst. A move of this magnitude typically requires a direct trigger such as an earnings beat, major contract announcement, or strategic update, none of which appear in the available news.

INTU dropped 20% on 6.5x normal volume with no news supplied to explain the move. A decline of this magnitude — pushing RSI to 28.7 with a bearish MACD cross — typically requires a major catalyst such as a major earnings miss, a guidance cut, or a macro-sector shock, but none of those are present in the available news set.

ARM is up 16% today with no supplied news to explain the move. The 30% 5-day run and elevated relative volume suggest a sustained momentum burst — possibly driven by options flow, AI/semiconductor sector rotation, or short covering — but none of these can be confirmed from the available information.

APLD surged 21.3% today on elevated but not extreme volume (1.5x relative), yet no news items were supplied to explain the move. The catalyst could be options flow, short covering, or sector rotation into AI/data center names, but none of that is supported by the available inputs.

ENPH is up 17% today on nearly double its average volume, but no news items were supplied to explain the move. The surge — extending a 30% five-day run — could reflect short-covering (ENPH has historically carried heavy short interest), sector rotation into solar/clean energy names, or options-driven momentum, none of which are evidenced in the provided news.

EVC surged 16% on nearly double its average volume with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. The spike into overbought RSI territory (75) suggests aggressive buying, possibly driven by options flow, short covering, or sector rotation — none of which are visible in the available data.

FCEL surged 30% on more than double its average volume with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. This type of action in a small-cap fuel cell stock is consistent with short squeeze dynamics, options flow, or retail momentum — none of which are visible in the available data.

INFQ surged 31.6% on volume 7x the norm, but the only available news is a low-impact Benzinga watchlist piece that groups it with several other quantum/tech names — this provides no specific catalyst for a move of this magnitude. The move is likely driven by options flow, retail momentum, or a sector-wide quantum computing rotation that isn't captured in the supplied news.

LAES surged ~14% on 2.79x relative volume with no identifiable news catalyst in the supplied feed. The move likely reflects retail-driven momentum, options flow, or short-squeeze dynamics in this small-cap name, but nothing in the available news explains the magnitude or timing.

QBTS surged 33% on 2.5x normal volume with no high-impact news directly tied to the company. The only supplied news is a low-impact Benzinga watchlist roundup that groups QBTS with other quantum computing names — this is insufficient to explain a move of this magnitude. The most plausible drivers are sector rotation into quantum computing stocks, sympathy momentum from peers like Rigetti, or significant options flow, none of which are evidenced in the supplied news.

QUBT surged ~19.5% on 2.47x relative volume with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. The spike could reflect options flow, short squeeze dynamics, or sector rotation into quantum computing names, but none of those mechanisms are confirmed by available information.

RGTI surged 30.65% on nearly 4x normal volume with no high-impact news directly tied to the company. The only news available is a low-impact roundup article mentioning Rigetti alongside several other names, which does not provide a company-specific catalyst to account for a move of this magnitude. The likely drivers — sector rotation into quantum computing names, options flow, or momentum chasing — are not visible in the supplied news.

SLS dropped 15.77% on 3.19x relative volume with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. The sell-off could reflect options expiration dynamics, a halt/resume, clinical or regulatory developments not yet captured, or institutional repositioning — but none of that can be confirmed from available data.

SPOT surged 13% on 3.5x normal volume with no news items supplied to explain the move. The magnitude and volume spike suggest a specific catalyst — likely an earnings beat, guidance raise, or analyst upgrade — but none is available in the input data to confirm.

WOLF surged ~19% with no news in the supplied feed to explain it. The move could reflect short covering, options flow, or sector rotation into SiC semiconductor names, but none of that is supported by the available data. Relative volume was actually below average (0.78x), which makes a clean fundamental catalyst less likely.

INTC is down 0.4% on below-average volume (relative volume 0.44), with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. The modest decline after a 5-day gain of 2.26% looks like routine profit-taking or low-conviction drift rather than any fundamental catalyst.

KO slipped 0.5% on below-average volume (rel. volume 0.91) with no news catalyst in the supplied feed. The move is consistent with routine sector rotation or broad market drift rather than any company-specific development.

NVDA is down 1.79% on above-average volume with a bearish MACD cross, extending a nearly 7% five-day slide. Neither news item directly explains the move — the Taiwan arms-sale pause affects defense procurement, not semiconductor supply chains, and the analyst commentary is commentary, not a catalyst. The selloff is more consistent with broader tech rotation or options/flow dynamics not captured in the supplied news.

RKLB dropped 6.5% on 1.39x relative volume with no news in the supplied feed to explain the move. The sell-off could reflect profit-taking after a strong prior run (RSI still elevated at 64.7), options flow, or broader sector rotation, but none of those are confirmed by available information.

XOM slipped 0.65% on slightly elevated volume (1.27x average) with no identifiable news catalyst. The move is likely attributable to broad energy sector rotation, crude oil price fluctuations, or general market flows rather than any company-specific development.
